ksas.statement: Ability to balance short-term operational data and AI demands against long-term strategic platform investments under resource and organizational constraints.
Ability to identify and resolve conflicts between data monetization objectives and data protection, privacy, and ethical obligations.
Ability to lead through organizational resistance to data and AI transformation, including navigating political dynamics, competing stakeholder interests, and cultural inertia.

Knowledge of enterprise data strategy frameworks, including data governance operating models, data architecture principles, and the organizational interdependencies that determine data program effectiveness.

Knowledge of AI governance structures, including model risk management, AI lifecycle oversight, accountability assignment, and the organizational conditions that enable responsible AI deployment at scale.

Knowledge of the regulatory and compliance landscape governing enterprise data and AI, including data protection law, AI-specific regulation, sector-specific obligations, and cross-jurisdictional compliance requirements.
Knowledge of data valuation and infonomics methodologies, including frameworks for quantifying data as an enterprise asset and connecting data investment to measurable financial outcomes.

Knowledge of organizational change management principles as applied to large-scale data and AI transformation programs, including adoption barriers, culture change levers, and executive sponsorship requirements.
Knowledge of enterprise risk management frameworks and how data risk, AI risk, and technology risk are integrated into enterprise risk reporting and board-level risk governance.
Knowledge of vendor and third-party risk management as it applies to data platform providers, AI model suppliers, cloud infrastructure vendors, and data service providers.
Skill in communicating data and AI strategy, risk posture, and investment priorities to board-level and C-suite audiences using financial and business terms rather than technical language.
Skill in constructing and defending data and AI investment portfolios using risk-adjusted financial frameworks, including expected value of information, NPV, and ROI analysis.
Skill in designing and operating enterprise data and AI governance councils, including role definition, decision rights, escalation paths, and policy enforcement mechanisms.
Skill in aligning data and AI program objectives to organizational strategy, translating business priorities into data and AI capability requirements with measurable outcomes.
Develop and maintain the enterprise data and AI strategy, aligned to business objectives, with defined investment priorities, milestones, accountability assignments, and success metrics.
Establish, communicate, and enforce enterprise-wide data and AI policies covering quality, privacy, security, ethics, and responsible use.
Report to the board, audit committee, and executive leadership on data and AI risk posture, program maturity, regulatory compliance status, and value realization.
Lead selection, contracting, and performance oversight of strategic data and AI technology vendors and platform providers.
Define and maintain the enterprise AI governance framework, including the AI system inventory, risk tiering, review thresholds, and decommissioning criteria.
Ability to prioritize data governance investments across competing data domains and business units with limited resources and uneven organizational maturity.
Knowledge of data governance operating models, including federated, centralized, and hub-and-spoke structures, and the organizational conditions under which each model is appropriate.
Knowledge of data catalog and metadata management platforms, including active metadata principles, automated lineage tracking, and business glossary governance.
Knowledge of master data management (MDM) principles, including golden record construction, data domain definition, MDM hub architectures, and stewardship workflows.
Knowledge of data quality management frameworks, including DQ dimensions (accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, uniqueness, validity), measurement approaches, and remediation workflows.
Knowledge of data maturity assessment frameworks, including DCAM, DAMA DMBOK, and CMMI-DMM, and how maturity scores translate to organizational investment priorities.
Skill in designing data stewardship programs that assign clear ownership, accountability, and service level agreements for enterprise data domains.
Skill in building and managing a Data Governance Council, including membership design, decision authority, meeting cadence, escalation processes, and conflict resolution.
Skill in communicating data quality and data trust issues to non-technical executive stakeholders in terms of business risk and operational impact.
Establish and operate the enterprise data governance program, including council structure, policy library, stewardship network, data domain ownership registry, and governance metrics.
Conduct enterprise data maturity assessments against recognized frameworks and define improvement roadmaps with measurable milestones and accountable owners.
Establish and maintain the enterprise data catalog, business glossary, and metadata management program, ensuring discoverability, lineage visibility, and classification currency.
Define and enforce enterprise data quality standards, including dimensional targets, measurement processes, issue triage procedures, and escalation thresholds.
Ability to distinguish between AI use cases that require formal governance review and those that can proceed under standard operating procedures, calibrating oversight to actual risk.
Knowledge of AI risk taxonomy, including model risk, algorithmic bias, opacity, adversarial vulnerability, data poisoning, training data integrity failures, and systemic AI failure modes.
Knowledge of responsible AI principles and their operationalization, including fairness metrics, explainability methods, human oversight requirements, and AI impact assessment methodologies.

Knowledge of AI regulatory frameworks, including the EU AI Act risk classification structure, conformity assessment requirements, prohibited use categories, and obligations on providers versus deployers.
Knowledge of AI model lifecycle management, including model versioning, drift detection, performance monitoring, retraining triggers, and decommissioning criteria.
Skill in designing AI governance frameworks that address the full AI lifecycle from procurement or build through development, testing, deployment, monitoring, and retirement.
Skill in constructing and communicating AI risk assessments to non-technical executive and board audiences using business risk framing and financial impact language.
Establish and maintain the enterprise AI inventory, cataloging all AI systems in production with risk tier, owner, training data provenance, model version, and review status.
Lead enterprise AI ethics reviews for high-stakes applications, including impact assessments, bias audits, and explainability documentation prior to production deployment.
Define the enterprise AI acceptable use policy, prohibited use categories, and human oversight requirements for AI-assisted decision making.
Establish post-deployment AI monitoring standards, including performance threshold definitions, drift detection protocols, incident reporting procedures, and remediation timelines.
Ability to sustain governance program momentum in organizations where data governance lacks executive mandate or where business unit resistance is high.
Knowledge of data governance program design, including operating model options, council structures, policy frameworks, stewardship network models, and escalation path architectures.
Knowledge of data policy authoring, including policy structure standards, enforcement mechanisms, exception handling processes, and policy lifecycle management.
Knowledge of data domain and data ownership concepts, including how to define domain boundaries, assign accountability, and resolve cross-domain ownership disputes.
Skill in operating data governance councils, including agenda management, issue escalation, decision documentation, and multi-stakeholder conflict resolution.
Skill in designing and running data stewardship programs, including steward selection, training, responsibility definition, and performance measurement.
Skill in measuring and reporting data governance program effectiveness using maturity scores, policy compliance rates, issue resolution metrics, and stewardship participation rates.
Author, maintain, and enforce the enterprise data policy library, ensuring policies are current, accessible, enforced, and aligned with regulatory requirements.
Operate the Data Governance Council, including scheduling, agenda preparation, issue tracking, decision logging, and follow-through on action items.
Manage the enterprise data stewardship network, including steward onboarding, role clarity, tools access, and regular steward community engagement.
Produce and distribute regular data governance program status reports, including maturity progress, open issues, policy violations, and upcoming regulatory obligations.
Ability to apply consistent governance standards across heterogeneous AI systems — from simple rule-based automation to large language models — without treating all systems identically.
Knowledge of AI governance framework components, including AI system registries, tiered review processes, impact assessment methodologies, and post-deployment monitoring requirements.
Knowledge of responsible AI principles and audit methods, including fairness metric selection, bias test design, explainability documentation standards, and human oversight protocols.
Knowledge of AI regulatory compliance requirements, including EU AI Act conformity assessment obligations, prohibited use categories, and documentation requirements for high-risk AI systems.
Skill in conducting AI impact assessments, including risk tiering, harm identification, bias evaluation, and documentation of human oversight mechanisms.
Skill in managing AI governance review processes, including intake, triage, stakeholder coordination, assessment execution, and decision documentation.
Maintain the enterprise AI system inventory, ensuring all AI applications are registered, risk-tiered, owned, and subject to appropriate review and monitoring.
Conduct AI impact assessments for new and materially modified AI systems prior to production deployment.
Manage ongoing AI policy compliance monitoring, including periodic review of deployed AI systems against current policy requirements and regulatory obligations.
Ability to serve as an effective bridge between business stakeholders who generate and consume data and technical stakeholders who store and process it.
Knowledge of the data domain for which stewardship responsibility is held, including its business purpose, system sources, consumer use cases, and applicable regulatory classifications.
Knowledge of data quality dimensions and how to assess, measure, and document quality issues within a specific data domain.
Knowledge of metadata standards, business glossary conventions, and data catalog tooling used to document and publish data asset definitions.
Skill in authoring and maintaining business glossary definitions, data dictionaries, and data asset documentation that are clear and accurate for both technical and business audiences.
Skill in identifying, documenting, and escalating data quality issues through established governance processes with appropriate business impact context.
Maintain the business glossary definitions, data asset catalog entries, and data lineage documentation for the assigned data domain.
Monitor data quality within the assigned domain, document issues, initiate remediation workflows, and track resolution to closure.
Participate in the data governance council and stewardship community, representing the assigned domain's interests and escalating unresolved issues through established channels.
Review and approve data access requests, data sharing agreements, and data use authorizations for data assets within the assigned domain.

Knowledge of data protection law, including GDPR, CCPA/CPRA, and sector-specific privacy regulations, with emphasis on the legal bases for processing, data subject rights, cross-border transfer mechanisms, and enforcement precedent.

Knowledge of the Data Protection Officer's statutory role, responsibilities, and independence requirements under GDPR Articles 37-39, including reporting lines, conflict-of-interest prohibitions, and the distinction between advisory and decision-making authority.

Knowledge of Data Protection Impact Assessment methodology, including the conditions that make assessments mandatory, techniques for evaluating risk to individuals, and how to document residual risk and mitigation measures to supervisory authority standards.

Knowledge of privacy-by-design and privacy-by-default principles, including their application to system architecture, data minimization, purpose limitation, storage limitation, and consent management design patterns.

Skill in conducting and reviewing Data Protection Impact Assessments, identifying processing activities that present high risk to individuals, and recommending proportionate technical and organizational measures to mitigate identified risks.

Skill in advising on data processing agreements, standard contractual clauses, binding corporate rules, and other cross-border data transfer mechanisms, ensuring compliance with applicable jurisdictional requirements and supervisory guidance.

Monitor organizational compliance with data protection obligations, conduct internal audits of processing activities, maintain the register of processing activities, and report compliance posture to senior management and supervisory authorities as required.

Serve as the designated point of contact for supervisory authorities and data subjects exercising their rights, managing inquiries, access requests, erasure requests, and complaints within statutory timeframes.

Advise on data protection implications of new projects, systems, and processing activities, ensuring privacy considerations are integrated into design and procurement decisions before processing begins.
